summary: A Southeast Asian empire (802-1431 CE) centered on Angkor in present-day Cambodia, founded by Jayavarman II when he declared independence from Java/Sailendra influence and established the devaraja ("god-king") cult. Ruled by a long line of kings named Jayavarman and Suryavarman who held the title chakravartin, the empire is best known for constructing the temple complex of Angkor Wat (built by Suryavarman II, the world's largest religious complex) and the Bayon temple with its smiling face towers (built by the Buddhist king Jayavarman VII). It relied on massive water reservoirs called baray for irrigation, was described by the Chinese diplomat Zhou Daguan, and eventually declined amid invasions from the Ayutthaya Kingdom.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: The empire is most often identified through its capital at Angkor in modern-day Cambodia and its construction of Angkor Wat. Its rulers held the titles chakravartin ("universal ruler") and devaraja ("god-king"), and it emerged from the earlier Funan and Chenla kingdoms that occupied the same region. At its territorial height it conquered the Mon Kingdom and Grahi, with its western border meeting the Pagan Kingdom. Southeast Asian empires like the Khmer are described by historians as operating under a "mandala model" of political power, coined by O.W. Wolters, in which the state tightly controlled its core territory but delegated rule of frontier regions to vassals.

Southeast Asian empires such as the Khmer tended to operate under this model of political power, in which the state exercises strong control over its core territory but delegates rule of frontier regions to vassals.

works.name: Founding: Jayavarman II & the Devaraja Cult
works.description: The empire's founding is credited to Jayavarman II, who declared independence from Java/the Sailendra dynasty around 802 (or 804) CE and established the devaraja "god-king" ruler cult after tutelage at a site on Phnom Kulen ("Lychee Mountain"). He absorbed the pre-existing Chenla people and moved his capital north of the Tonle Sap, removing the kingdom from Srivijaya's influence.

works.name: Hariharalaya & the Roluos Group
works.description: The empire's "most prolific leader" (traditionally identified as Indravarman I) established a capital at Roluos, named Hariharalaya for the deity Harihara, where he ordered construction of a namesake group of temples with monasteries — the ruins now known as the Roluos Group. Some questions instead attribute the Roluos temples to the empire's founder himself.

Recent research on the slow decline of this polity supports Bernard-Philippe Groslier's claim that the decline resulted from overdevelopment of a "hydraulic city." The first capital of this polity, named for the deity Harihara, houses early examples of "temple mountains."

works.name: Angkor Wat & Suryavarman II
works.description: Suryavarman II, the empire's greatest ruler, ordered the construction of Angkor Wat, the world's largest religious complex, designed to represent the axis mundi Mount Meru with five towers and Ramayana-inspired bas-reliefs depicting an army he led. His reign marked a switch from Shiva to Vishnu worship, and he invaded Champa while restoring diplomatic and commercial relations with China and the Chola Empire. Angkor Wat later became a national symbol, appearing on Cambodia's flag and undergoing restoration under both Jayavarman VII and, centuries later, King Norodom (Sihanouk).

works.name: Bayon & Jayavarman VII
works.description: The Buddhist king Jayavarman VII built the Bayon temple at the capital, covered with face towers depicting a smiling king and "all-seeing eyes" that promoted the devaraja concept of divine kingship. He was repeatedly depicted as the bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara, patronized Mahayana Buddhism, and revived Buddhism by building sites like Ta Prohm and Neak Pean. He is credited with rebuilding the capital, including Angkor Wat, after it was sacked by the Chams.

works.name: Baray Reservoirs & the Hydraulic City
works.description: The empire relied on massive artificial reservoirs called baray to irrigate its land and supply its cities with water, part of an extensive canal system managed from the capital at Angkor. Their east-west orientation has led some scholars to suggest a spiritual, not just practical, purpose, and the empire is cited as evidence against August Wittfogel's "hydraulic empire" thesis because its irrigation was controlled by monks rather than the state.

works.name: Zhou Daguan's Account
works.description: The Yuan-dynasty Chinese official Zhou Daguan, who served as an ambassador under the Chengzong Emperor, left a first-person account of the empire's customs after visiting its capital. He described commoners living in thatched-roof houses without tables, chairs, or bowls and wearing only loincloths, noted that women dominated all market trade, and recorded an elaborate royal procession and the once-gold-covered towers of Bayon.

works.name: Society, Religion & Legends
works.description: The empire's society was organized into a caste system of sdech (royalty), neamoeun montrey (nobility), and reastr (peasantry), and practiced a conflict-resolution method called "celestial judgment" in which disputing parties sat in stone towers until one fell ill. It produced martial legends of usurper-kings, including a former cucumber farmer and a man who seized power by killing a rival astride a war elephant, as well as the martial art bokator and thousands of hermit-carved phallus-shaped carvings on the Kulen Plateau.

works.name: Decline & Collapse
works.description: The empire's fall is attributed to invasions by the Ayutthaya Kingdom, and historian Bernard-Philippe Groslier argued the underlying decline stemmed from overdevelopment of Angkor as a "hydraulic city" — a claim recent research on the drying out of its barays has supported. The Champa kingdom sacked the capital at Angkor roughly thirty years after Suryavarman II's conquest of it, and after the empire's collapse, its territory passed through periods named for the cities of Chaktomuk and Longvek.

works.name: Rediscovery & Historiography
works.description: Western interest in the empire's ruins was spurred by Henri Mouhot's 19th-century expedition, which "discovered" Angkor despite it being known to native Cambodians for centuries (following an earlier visit by Antonio da Madalena). Etienne Aymonier was the first archaeologist to systematically study the empire, George Coedes compiled an 8-volume transcription of its inscriptions while working for the EFEO, and Roland Fletcher of the University of Sydney later used remote sensing technology to rediscover the empire's canals.

works.name: Legacy
works.description: The empire's name lives on in the modern Khmer people and gives its name to the 20th-century Communist Party of Kampuchea, better known as the Khmer Rouge, which ruled Cambodia under Pol Pot — a distinct, much later historical topic.

clues.clue: The empire gives its name to the 20th-century communist party (the Khmer Rouge) led by Pol Pot
examples: name this empire whose land now comprises much of Cambodia, and which gives its name to a Communist party led by Pol Pot.

comprehensive_summary: The Khmer Empire (802-1431 CE) was a Southeast Asian empire centered on Angkor in present-day Cambodia, having emerged from the earlier Funan and Chenla kingdoms. It was founded by Jayavarman II, who declared independence from Java/the Sailendra dynasty around 802 (or 804) CE, established the devaraja "god-king" cult after receiving tutelage at a site on Phnom Kulen ("Lychee Mountain"), absorbed the pre-existing Chenla people, and moved his capital north of the Tonle Sap to escape Srivijayan influence; an inscription (K. 235) and legendary accounts credit him and his line with founding the god-king concept, and the founder is said to have established 121 "Houses of Fire" as rest stops for travelers. The empire's rulers held the titles chakravartin ("universal ruler") and devaraja, and operated under what historian O.W. Wolters called a "mandala" model of overlapping, decentralized authority. The empire's most prolific early leader established a new capital at Roluos (Hariharalaya, named for the deity Harihara), building the namesake Roluos Group of temples and monasteries — though some sources also attribute these temples to the founder himself. At its territorial height, the empire conquered the Mon Kingdom and Grahi, bordering the Pagan Kingdom to the west. Its greatest ruler, Suryavarman II, ordered construction of Angkor Wat — the world's largest religious complex, designed as a Vaishnavite temple modeled on the axis mundi Mount Meru with five towers and Ramayana-inspired bas-reliefs depicting his own army — marking a religious shift from Shiva to Vishnu worship. Suryavarman II invaded Champa, restored diplomatic and commercial relations with China, and exchanged a jewel with the Chola Kingdom then ruled by Kulottunga I. About thirty years after his conquest of Champa, the Chams sacked the Khmer capital; it was later rebuilt, and Angkor Wat restored, under the Buddhist king Jayavarman VII, who also built the Bayon temple, covered in "face towers" depicting a smiling king whose all-seeing eyes promoted the devaraja idea of divine kingship. Jayavarman VII was repeatedly depicted as the bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara, patronized Mahayana Buddhism, and revived Buddhism with sites like Ta Prohm and Neak Pean; Bayon's bas-reliefs depict a naval battle against the Chams, and its towers were once gold-covered. The empire relied on massive artificial reservoirs called baray, fed by the Tonle Sap, for irrigation and water supply; their east-west orientation has suggested a spiritual purpose to some scholars, and two barays were built near a sandstone complex depicting the Churning of the Ocean of Milk. The empire is cited as evidence against August Wittfogel's "hydraulic empire" thesis, since its irrigation was controlled by monks rather than the state. The Yuan-dynasty Chinese official Zhou Daguan, an ambassador under the Chengzong Emperor, left a first-person account of the empire's customs: commoners lived in thatched-roof houses without tables, chairs, or bowls and wore only loincloths, women dominated market trade, and he described an elaborate royal procession, gold-covered towers at Bayon, and a moated city guarded by a statue of a nine-headed snake. Khmer society was organized into a caste system of sdech (royalty), neamoeun montrey (nobility), and reastr (peasantry), and practiced a "celestial judgment" conflict-resolution ritual in which disputants sat in stone towers until one fell ill. Legendary rulers include a former cucumber farmer who assassinated his predecessor and married his daughter to seize the throne, an Oedipus-like king said to have built the first capital of black sandstone, and a usurper who killed a rival by jumping onto his war elephant's head; a ruler built a seven-tier pyramid at Koh Ker, another banned talking to trees on Kulen Mountain as a conservation measure, and hermits carved thousands of phallus-shaped carvings into limestone on the Kulen Plateau. The empire developed the martial art of bokator, depicted on temple friezes. The empire's decline is attributed to invasions from the Ayutthaya Kingdom; historian Bernard-Philippe Groslier argued the underlying cause was overdevelopment of Angkor as a "hydraulic city," and the drying out of its barays is cited as a major cause of the collapse. After its fall, its territory passed through periods named for the cities of Chaktomuk and Longvek. Western interest in the ruins was spurred by Henri Mouhot's 19th-century expedition (following an earlier visit by Antonio da Madalena); Etienne Aymonier was the first archaeologist to systematically study the empire, George Coedes compiled an 8-volume transcription of its inscriptions for the EFEO, and Roland Fletcher of the University of Sydney later used remote sensing to rediscover its canals. The empire's name lives on in the modern Khmer people and gives its name to the 20th-century Khmer Rouge, the communist party led by Pol Pot that ruled Cambodia from 1975-1979 — a distinct, much later historical topic.
recursive_suggestions: Khmer Rouge — distinct 20th-century topic (Pol Pot's communist regime, 1975-1979, killing fields, S-21); excluded 9 tossups here that were actually about this regime or modern Cambodia during/after it, not the medieval empire
Angkor Wat — could become its own standalone Fine Arts/Architecture or History topic given its density of independent clues
Bayon — could become its own standalone topic (face towers, Jayavarman VII, Avalokiteshvara depictions) if it accumulates more independent clue presence
Zhou Daguan — recurring primary-source answerline; watch for confusion since he is sometimes the direct answer rather than the Khmer Empire
Jayavarman VII — recurring individual ruler answerline (prompted separately from 'Jayavarman' in some bonuses); could merit a standalone figure page
Suryavarman II — recurring individual ruler answerline, distinct from the empire itself in several bonus parts
Champa Kingdom — recurring neighboring/rival kingdom, appears as its own answerline in bonus parts about the Cham sack of Angkor
